reasoning: All four correctly locate the dispute in the unobserved counterfactual and the garden of forking paths rather than in the raw data, and all converge on quasi-experiments (DiD with pre-trends, RDD, synthetic control) plus pre-registration as the settling evidence. C edges ahead by naming the full slate of rival causes explicitly (pre-existing trend, confounder, reverse causation, regression to the mean), elevating the substitution heuristic ('who is biased' replacing 'what does the evidence support'), and stating the strongest discriminators precisely (pre-trends, dose-response, out-of-sample replication as the one thing a story tuned to this decade cannot be reshaped to fit), with a sharply actionable conditional verdict. B matches C on calibration and adds the incommensurability/incommensurable-frames point and the symmetric-bias-as-useless-tiebreaker insight, but is marginally less crisp on ranking the evidence hierarchy. A is tight and well-organized with a clean strength-ranked evidence ladder and the 'report what each camp assumed' move, but covers slightly less ground on alternative explanations. D is solid and concrete (the wage-subsidy worked example is good) but reads more as a competent checklist with less structural framing of why the split is symmetric and less depth on bias mechanics.
